dataset.title: Global Urban Tree Species (GUTS): Revealing tree species diversity across the world’s urban areas

creator.organizationName: Department of Earth System Science, Tsinghua University

abstract.para: Diverse tree communities can bolster urban ecosystem resilience and provide vital ecosystem services. However, existing urban tree species datasets have limited geographic coverage and contain inadequate attributes. To address those gaps, we developed the Global Urban Tree Species (GUTS) dataset by integrating data from literature, biodiversity databases, and other open sources. The new dataset encompasses 159,845 occurrence records of 10,094 tree species in 8,349 cities and 139 countries. Among them, 109,879 records were confirmed from urban areas, representing 11.18% of global tree species diversity. The dataset has been validated using multiple methods. GUTS fills critical data gaps and provides a foundation for future research and management of global urban biodiversity.

intellectualRights.para: This work is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. To view a copy of this license, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/.

geographicCoverage.geographicDescription: We developed the Global Urban Tree Species (GUTS) dataset by integrating data from literature, biodiversity databases, and other open sources. The new dataset encompasses 159,845 occurrence records of 10,094 tree species in 8,349 cities and 139 countries. Notable urban areas covered in the dataset include major cities like New York, USA; São Paulo, Brazil; London, UK; Sydney, Australia; and Beijing, China.

taxonomicCoverage.generalTaxonomicCoverage: All tree species were identified to species level using authoritative taxonomic databases such as World Flora Online, Catalogue of Life, Tropicos, and GBIF. The dataset includes records from 139 countries and 8,349 cities, covering 10,094 tree species across 227 families and 2,102 genera. Among these, the urban area data cover 115 countries and 7,020 cities, representing 6,450 species (approximately 11.18% of global tree species listed in Global Tree Search (GTS) v.1.8), belonging to 200 families (about 71.94% of global families listed in GTS v.1.8) and 1,639 genera (about 39.30% of global genera listed in GTS v.1.8).

description.para: Following the PRISMA protocol, we conducted a comprehensive literature search from August 1975 to March 12th, 2024, across six databases: Google Scholar, Scopus, Web of Science, CNKI, Wanfang Data, and CSTJ. The search strings included combinations of terms “urban or city”, “tree or woody plant or vegetation or forest”, and “species or diversity”. The search yielded a total of 112,189 publications, which were reduced to 84,785 after removing duplicates. We initially screened titles and abstracts for relevant studies on urban tree species, retaining 3,434 articles. After excluding 47 inaccessible articles, 3,387 publications underwent full-text review. We applied two selection criteria: (1) studies had to be based on field surveys, excluding reviews or historical records, and (2) surveys needed to cover multiple locations, excluding single-site studies. This resulted in 1,386 publications (1978–2024) meeting the criteria.

From these, we extracted species names, locations, sample sizes, and bibliographic details. Two researchers independently reviewed the data, resolving any discrepancies through discussion or with input from a senior researcher. The species lists were then reviewed by plant taxonomy experts.

We queried major biodiversity databases using a compiled list of accepted tree species names from GlobalTreeSearch 1.8 (GTS) and several taxonomy databases. GlobalTreeSearch, the first authoritative global list of tree species, served as the primary source, supplemented by nomenclature from World Flora Online (WFO, version 2023.12), Catalogue of Life, Tropicos, and GBIF. This list facilitated later data extraction and taxonomic cleaning. Taxonomic information from WFO was downloaded from its webpage, while data from Catalogue of Life and Tropicos were obtained using the R package taxize, and from GBIF using the R package taxizedb.

We used this species list to query eight biodiversity databases: GBIF, BIEN, Atlas of Living Australia, BioTime, RAINBIO, iDigBio, SpeciesLink, and BISON. These queries returned over 100 million records. After merging records by species names, geographic coordinates, country, region codes, and collection years, we obtained 30,242,556 occurrence records, with 27,787,806 (91.9%) including geographic coordinates, covering 57,871 tree species.

Field Surveys: Additional data were collected through field surveys conducted in five Chinese cities. Survey methods included systematic sampling and direct observation, with species identification verified by taxonomists.

The data processing includes three steps: data cleansing, urban species occurrence identification, and standardization. At the cleansing stage, query results were cleaned using a protocol which can address taxonomic and geographical errors in occurrence records. The process involves cleaning taxonomic errors, correcting geographical errors, and applying quality labeling. For urban occurrence identification, we utilized land cover data provided by the European Space Agency (ESA), alongside the Global Human Settlement Layer (GHSL) and Global Urban Boundaries (GUB) datasets, ensuring precise extraction of species records within urban areas. Finally, the data were standardized by cross-referencing species names with global taxonomic resources and harmonizing city names using a comprehensive geographical database, ensuring that the final dataset is both consistent and reliable.

The dataset encompasses urba areas across 139 countries, including 8349 cities. Data were collected from literature databases, biodiversity databases, and field surveys, ensuring comprehensive geographic coverage and representation of urban tree biodiversity.

samplingDescription.para: Data collection involved systematic searches of scientific literature, extraction of relevant urban tree species data, and verification through field surveys. Geographic coordinates were validated using GIS tools and urban boundary datasets. Multiple researchers conducted cross-checks to ensure data accuracy and consistency.
